quiz.title: Unit 10 Quiz 2, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Solubility Calculations - Saturation and Precipitation
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Solubility, Saturated/Unsaturated/Supersaturated Solutions, Percent Saturation, Precipitation Calculations, Mass-Mass Conversions

questions.question: A saturated solution of NaCl is prepared with 100 g of water. How many grams of NaCl are present if the solubility is 36 g/100 g water?
questions.explanation:
In a saturated solution, the amount dissolved equals the solubility.
Mass NaCl = 36 g

questions.question: Which of the following is FALSE?
options: The solubility of most solid solutes increases with temperature
The solubility of most gases increases with temperature
Unsaturated solutions contain less solute than the solubility limit
Some solids remain undissolved in supersaturated solutions
questions.explanation: The solubility of most gases DECREASES with increasing temperature, not increases. This is why carbonated drinks go flat when warm. The other statements are true: most solids do increase in solubility with temperature; unsaturated solutions have less solute than the solubility limit; and supersaturated solutions are unstable and often have excess solid precipitate out.

questions.question: If 30 g of KNO₃ is dissolved in 50 g of water at 25°C (solubility = 32 g/100 g water), determine whether the solution is saturated, unsaturated, or supersaturated.
questions.explanation:
Convert to per 100 g water: 30 g/50 g = 60 g/100 g water
60 g > 32 g → supersaturated

questions.question: The solubility of NH₄Cl at 50°C is 60 g/100 g water. How many grams are required to make a saturated solution in 120 g of water?
80 g of Na₂SO₄ is dissolved in 200 g of water at 25°C. Solubility = 47 g/100 g water. How much will precipitate?
questions.explanation:
Maximum that can dissolve = 47 g/100 g × 200 g = 94 g
80 g is less than 94 g → all dissolves, precipitate = 0 g

questions.question: A solution contains 0.5 mol of Ca(OH)₂ in 1 L of water. The solubility is 0.02 mol/L. How much precipitates?
questions.explanation:
Maximum that can dissolve = 0.02 mol
Moles that precipitate = 0.5 - 0.02 = 0.48 mol
Molar mass Ca(OH)₂ = 74.10 g/mol
Mass precipitate = 0.48 × 74.10 = 35.6 g

questions.question: 25 g of sugar is dissolved in 100 g of water at 20°C. Solubility = 26 g/100 g water. Calculate the percent saturation.
The solubility of NaNO₃ at 25°C is 87 g/100 g water. How many grams can dissolve in 400 g of water?
10 g of KCl is dissolved in 25 g of water at 25°C. Solubility = 34 g/100 g water. Determine percent saturation.
questions.explanation:
Convert to per 100 g: 10 g/25 g = 40 g/100 g water
Percent saturation = (40 / 34) × 100% = 118%

questions.question: A solution contains 45 g of NaCl in 150 g of water at 25°C. Solubility = 36 g/100 g water. How many grams of precipitate will form?
questions.explanation:
Maximum that can dissolve = 36 g/100 g × 150 g = 54 g
45 g is less than 54 g → all dissolves, precipitate = 0 g

questions.question: The solubility of AgNO₃ at 25°C is 216 g/100 g water. How many moles can dissolve in 500 g of water?
questions.explanation:
Mass AgNO₃ that can dissolve = 216 g/100 g × 500 g = 1080 g
Molar mass AgNO₃ = 169.87 g/mol
Moles AgNO₃ = 1080 / 169.87 = 6.36 mol

questions.question: 60 g of Na₂CO₃ is added to 250 g of water at 25°C. Solubility = 21 g/100 g water. Calculate grams of precipitate formed.
questions.explanation:
Maximum that can dissolve = 21 g/100 g × 250 g = 52.5 g
Mass precipitate = 60 - 52.5 = 7.5 g

questions.question: A solution contains 15 g of NaCl in 50 g of water at 25°C. Solubility = 36 g/100 g water. What is the percent saturation?
questions.explanation:
Convert to per 100 g: 15 g/50 g = 30 g/100 g water
Percent saturation = (30 / 36) × 100% = 83.3%

questions.question: 100 g of water contains 40 g of KNO₃ at 25°C. Solubility = 32 g/100 g water. Calculate grams that will precipitate.
A solution contains 0.1 mol of CaSO₄ in 500 mL of water. The solubility of CaSO₄ is 0.08 mol/L. How much will precipitate?
questions.explanation:
Volume = 500 mL = 0.5 L
Maximum moles that can dissolve = 0.08 mol/L × 0.5 L = 0.04 mol
Moles that precipitate = 0.1 - 0.04 = 0.06 mol
Molar mass CaSO₄ = 136.14 g/mol
Mass precipitate = 0.06 × 136.14 = 8.17 g

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered solubility and precipitation calculations! 🧪💧
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review converting concentrations to per 100 g water basis.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on calculating maximum soluble mass first.
scoreMessages.0: Please review solubility concepts, saturation types, and precipitation calculations.
